title: AI Chips Drive Around A Third Of TSMC Revenues
gist: TSMC's Q2 2026 results show record revenue of $40.2 billion (net income up 74.7% YoY) as AI chips likely drove roughly a third of total revenue and about half of HPC segment revenue; the company is adding $100 billion more for Arizona capacity (total $265 billion in the US) and raising 2026 capex to $60-64 billion while deliberately keeping supply tighter than demand because shortages are more profitable.

insights.claim: TSMC is adding $100 billion to expand Arizona fab capacity, bringing total committed US investment to $265 billion, with four more Arizona fabs focused on 2 nanometer and smaller nodes.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Confirms TSMC is betting its capex cycle heavily on US-based advanced-node capacity, a multi-year tailwind for US fab equipment, construction, and power/utility suppliers tied to Arizona.
insights.quote: TSMC announced during the call that it was adding another $100 billion to expand capacity in its Arizona fabs, which brings the total investment in its US operations to $265 billion over several years.
insights.locator: And it is big money indeed.

insights.claim: TSMC raised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance from an earlier $52-56 billion range to $60-64 billion, which at the high end represents a 56.5% increase in annual capex versus 2025's $40.9 billion.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A capex guidance raise of this magnitude signals TSMC sees AI demand accelerating beyond its own January forecast, a leading indicator for semiconductor equipment makers (ASML, Applied Materials, etc.) and for the durability of the AI infrastructure buildout.
insights.quote: Earlier this year, TSMC expected to pump $52 billion to $56 billion into capex, and now that has been boosted to somewhere between $60 billion to $64 billion.
insights.locator: To give you some perspective, TSMC spent $29.8 billion in capex in 2024

insights.claim: The author estimates roughly less than half of TSMC's capex increase for 2026 stems from inflation in facility and equipment prices caused by demand exceeding supply, with the remainder being genuine capacity expansion.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Investors modeling TSMC capex-to-capacity ratios should discount headline capex growth for input-cost inflation, not just physical wafer capacity added.
insights.quote: My guess is that slightly less than half of the increase in capex is from inflation, and the rest is for actually capacity expansion beyond the original plan.
insights.locator: Some of this money is for increased capacity for 2026

insights.claim: It takes TSMC five to seven years to build a fab, per CEO CC Wei, meaning committed capex spending is spread out and lags far behind announced investment totals.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Investors should not expect headline mega-investment figures (like the $265 billion US commitment) to translate into near-term revenue or capacity; actual capacity comes online on a multi-year lag.
insights.quote: Wei reminded everyone that it takes five to seven years to build these fabs, by the way. So it does not happen overnight.
insights.locator: A lot of this driven by AMD's "Venice" Epyc 9006 CPUs

insights.claim: The author's own model estimates TSMC's AI training and inference chip revenue at $13.31 billion in Q2 2026, up 68.3% year on year, which is faster growth than the mid-to-high 50 percent CAGR TSMC itself guided for 2026-2030.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If AI chip revenue is tracking well above TSMC's own multi-year CAGR guidance, actual AI hardware demand may be outpacing even bullish public forecasts, supporting continued strength in AI silicon supply-chain names.
insights.quote: My best guess is that AI training and inference chips manufactured by TSMC represented $13.31 billion in Q2 2026, up 68.3 percent and way faster than that mid-to-high 50 percent CAGR in the forecast that TSMC provided earlier this year.
insights.locator: What everyone wants to know is how much AI is driving sales at TSMC.

insights.claim: AI chips likely accounted for about a third of TSMC's total Q2 2026 revenue and roughly half of its HPC segment revenue, based on the author's independent estimate since TSMC stopped disclosing this breakdown.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Establishes a quantified baseline for how AI-dependent TSMC's revenue has become, useful for stress-testing TSMC valuation against any AI capex slowdown scenario.
insights.quote: If my model is right, AI products represented about a third of overall TSMC revenues and about half of HPC segment revenues.
insights.locator: What everyone wants to know is how much AI is driving sales at TSMC.

insights.claim: TSMC's HPC segment (datacenter XPUs, server CPUs, switch ASICs, FPGAs, desktop/laptop chips) generated $26.53 billion in Q2 2026, up 47.1% year on year, and now far exceeds smartphone segment revenue of $8.84 billion (up 8.9% year on year, down 5.2% sequentially).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Confirms the structural shift at TSMC from a smartphone-driven foundry to a datacenter/AI-driven one, changing which end markets matter most for TSMC's growth trajectory and cyclicality.
insights.quote: the HPC segment drove $26.53 billion in sales, up 47.1 percent year on year and dominated by server CPUs and datacenter XPUs.
insights.locator: In the meantime, that "HPC" business at TSMC

insights.claim: TSMC reported Q2 2026 revenue of $40.2 billion (up 33.7% YoY, up 12% sequentially) and net income of $22.37 billion (up 74.7% YoY), with net income representing 55.6% of revenue.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Net income growing more than twice as fast as revenue signals expanding margins driven by advanced-node pricing power, a key data point for TSMC's earnings quality and pricing leverage in AI silicon.
insights.quote: TSMC reported record revenues of $40.2 billion, up 33.7 percent year on year and up 12 percent sequentially from Q1 2026. But net income grew 2.2X faster, up 74.7 percent year on year to $22.37 billion.
insights.locator: In the June quarter, TSMC reported record revenues

insights.claim: TSMC's revenue per 12-inch-equivalent wafer reached $9,271 in Q2 2026, up 14.6% year on year and 7.8% sequentially, and is roughly double what it was four years ago and triple what it was seven years ago, reflecting the shift to more expensive advanced nodes under supply-constrained pricing.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Rising per-wafer pricing under persistent supply constraints is a direct driver of TSMC margin expansion and signals continued cost inflation for chip buyers (Nvidia, AMD, hyperscalers) reliant on advanced nodes.
insights.quote: Revenue per wafer came to $9,271, up 14.6 percent year on year but up 7.8 percent sequentially. Four years ago, the revenue per wafer was half as much, if you can believe it, and seven years ago, revenue per wafer was a third of what it is today.
insights.locator: In the second quarter ended in June, wafer shipments

insights.claim: TSMC appears to be deliberately keeping AI chip supply somewhat below demand because shortages are more profitable, as evidenced by margin growth outpacing revenue growth even as customers clamor for more capacity.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If true, this implies AI chip shortages are partly a monopolist's pricing strategy rather than a pure physical constraint, meaning downstream AI hardware costs may stay elevated longer than a purely supply-driven model would predict, and TSMC's margins have more room before competition forces prices down.
insights.quote: You can see why TSMC might not be in such a hurry to have demand met perfectly by supply. Shortages are more profitable. The trick is to not be too short.
insights.locator: In the June quarter, TSMC reported record revenues

insights.claim: CEO CC Wei says demand growth guidance keeps strengthening quarter over quarter but declined to give an updated numeric CAGR for AI business growth during the July 2026 earnings call, despite reaffirming a January 2026 forecast of mid-to-high 50 percent CAGR from 2026-2030.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: TSMC management's own demand signal is trending up faster than public guidance, suggesting the January CAGR forecast may prove conservative and could be revised upward in coming quarters.
insights.quote: Wei would not give an updated number during his call with Wall Street today, but he did say the number was increasing and the CAGR is getting "stronger and stronger and stronger."
insights.locator: At the moment, CC Wei, the foundry's chief executive officer

insights.claim: CC Wei described the difficulty of forecasting demand: aggregating every customer's individually truthful but competitively-inflated demand numbers does not produce an accurate collective truth, requiring TSMC to apply significant judgment to capacity planning.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Highlights structural risk that reported hyperscaler/AI chip demand signals are systematically inflated by competitive dynamics, meaning capacity build-out plans across the industry could be running ahead of true end demand.
insights.quote: So I put all together, all the truths together is not a truth. Mark down that word. So yes, we do a very careful judgment. It may not be correct, but we did it carefully because this is a big money.
insights.locator: Wei joked a bit about how fuzzy all of these numbers are

insights.claim: AMD's upcoming 'Venice' Epyc 9006 CPUs and MI450 GPUs are driving much of the ramp on TSMC's 2 nanometer node, which recognized its first-ever revenue in this quarter at $1.21 billion, with AMD playing the same node-pioneering role Nvidia played on TSMC's 4 nanometer process years earlier.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD's early 2nm adoption positions it as the lead beneficiary/risk-bearer of new-node yield issues, and its MI450 GPU ramp (expected around AMD's Advancing AI 2026 event) is a near-term catalyst worth tracking against Nvidia's competitive response.
insights.quote: A lot of this driven by AMD's "Venice" Epyc 9006 CPUs and the MI450 GPUs that are expected later this year... someone has to drive the ramp to better yields and AMD is doing it now just as Nvidia did it with 4 nanometer TSMC processes a few years back.
insights.locator: A lot of this driven by AMD's "Venice" Epyc 9006 CPUs

insights.claim: Intel Foundry's EMIB-T interposer packaging technology is gaining traction and relieving pressure on TSMC's CoWoS packaging supply, which CC Wei said is an even tighter bottleneck than TSMC's chip etching capacity.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Advanced packaging, not just wafer etching, is the binding capacity constraint in AI chip supply chains; Intel Foundry gaining a packaging role for the industry is an underappreciated competitive foothold and diversification of the packaging bottleneck.
insights.quote: he was pleased that Intel was getting traction on its EMIB-T interposer technology because it took some of the heat of its CoWoS interposer packaging supply, which is even together than its chip etching supply.
insights.locator: My guess is that slightly less than half of the increase

insights.claim: China's SMIC currently holds about 6 percent of global foundry business and is expected to grow that share as China is largely locked out of using TSMC for advanced chips, but real competitive threats to TSMC from Intel Foundry, Samsung Foundry, and SMIC are still four to five years away.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: TSMC's near-monopoly pricing power on advanced nodes should persist for several more years, supporting continued margin strength, but investors should watch SMIC's domestic share gains as a longer-horizon geopolitical/competitive risk to TSMC's China-linked and advanced-node dominance.
insights.quote: China's Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corp is backed by the Chinese government and has 6 percent of the foundry business at the moment and will grow its share... TSMC will eventually have competition, just as Nvidia will. Take a very deep breath... it may take another four or five years.
insights.locator: In the June quarter, TSMC reported record revenues

insights.claim: Roughly 70 percent of TSMC's capex goes to front-end wafer fabrication across all chip types, 10-20 percent to packaging facilities, and the remaining 10-20 percent to specialty products, and CC Wei expects this ratio to hold going forward.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Gives investors a template for allocating TSMC's ~$60-64 billion 2026 capex across wafer fab equipment, packaging (CoWoS/COUPE), and specialty lines when modeling supplier beneficiaries.
insights.quote: In general, about 70 percent of this capex is for front-end chip wafer production for all kinds of chips... with 10 percent to 20 percent being allocated for chip packaging facilities.
insights.locator: My guess is that slightly less than half of the increase
